title: Dog Ate Sugar-Free Gum with Xylitol: Immediate Emergency Guide
meta_desc: If your dog ate gum containing xylitol, this is a life-threatening emergency. Learn why immediate veterinary care is required to save your pet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Xylitol is a highly toxic sugar substitute that causes life-threatening blood sugar drops and liver failure in dogs, requiring immediate emergency veterinary care. Symptoms can appear within 15 to 30 minutes; contact a vet immediately and bring product packaging if possible.

What is xylitol and why is it dangerous for my dog?

Xylitol is a common sugar substitute found in many sugar-free gums, candies, baked goods, and even some peanut butters. While perfectly safe for humans, it is extremely toxic to dogs. Even a very small amount can cause a rapid, life-threatening release of insulin in a dog's body.

Is xylitol ingestion a medical emergency for my pet?

  • Yes, this is a high-priority emergency requiring immediate contact with a veterinarian or emergency animal hospital.
  • Xylitol causes a sudden drop in blood sugar (hypoglycemia), which can lead to seizures.
  • In higher doses, it can lead to acute liver failure and death.
  • Symptoms can appear as quickly as 15 to 30 minutes after ingestion.

How does a photo of the product packaging help with veterinary triage?

  • Take a clear photo of the ingredients list and brand name if you still have the product packaging.
  • Show the photo to your veterinarian or triage assistant so they can calculate the exact concentration of xylitol ingested.
  • This information is critical for determining the level of toxicity and the specific treatment plan required for your dog.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, xylitol stimulates a rapid, dose-dependent insulin release, leading to profound hypoglycemia; dosages exceeding 75-100 mg/kg can cause hypoglycemia, while dosages greater than 500 mg/kg may result in severe hepatic insufficiency or failure. Clinical signs of hypoglycemia, including vomiting, weakness, ataxia, depression, hypokalemia, seizures, and coma, can appear within 30 minutes of ingestion or be delayed up to 12-18 hours, especially with gum products. Liver injury signs may manifest 24-48 hours post-ingestion, although liver enzyme increases can be detected within 8-12 hours. Emesis should only be attempted under veterinary supervision in asymptomatic animals, as activated charcoal is ineffective for xylitol. If a dog ingests >75-100 mg/kg of xylitol, hospitalization is warranted, with blood glucose monitored every 1-2 hours for at least 12 hours and liver values evaluated every 24 hours for 72 hours; hypoglycemia should be managed with IV dextrose boluses or constant-rate infusions until the dog maintains normal blood glucose without supplementation. Dogs ingesting >500 mg/kg should receive dextrose and hepatoprotectants like N-acetylcysteine, S-adenosylmethionine, and silymarin, and coagulopathy should be treated if present.

Chapter: Toxicology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2968)

faqs.question: How much xylitol is dangerous for a dog?
faqs.answer: As little as 0.1 grams per kilogram of body weight can cause hypoglycemia. For a small dog, even a single piece of gum can be a fatal dose.

faqs.question: What are the early signs of xylitol poisoning?
faqs.answer: Early symptoms include vomiting, followed by signs of low blood sugar such as lethargy, stumbling, weakness, or collapse.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Do not induce vomiting unless specifically instructed to do so by a veterinary professional, as this can be dangerous if the dog is already showing neurological symptoms.
